--- Page 1 ---
The Coordination Gap: Alternation Metrics for
Temporal Dynamics in Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes
Nikolaos Al. Papadopoulosa,∗, Konstantinos E. Psannisa
aDepartment of Applied Informatics, University of Macedonia, Egnatia 156, Thessaloniki
54636, Greece
Abstract
Multi-agent coordination dilemmas expose a fundamental tension between indi-
vidual optimization and collective welfare, yet characterizing such coordination
requires metrics sensitive to temporal structure and collective dynamics. As a
diagnostic testbed, we study a BoE-derived multi-agent variant of the Battle
of the Exes, formalizing it as a Markov game in which turn-taking emerges as
a periodic coordination regime. Conventional outcome-based metrics (e.g., ef-
ficiency and min/max fairness) are temporally blind—they cannot distinguish
structured alternation from monopolistic or random access patterns—and fair-
ness ratios lose discriminative power as n grows, obscuring inequities.
To address this limitation, we introduce Perfect Alternation (PA) as a ref-
erence coordination regime and propose six novel Alternation (ALT) metrics
designed as temporally sensitive observables of coordination quality.
Using
Q-learning agents as a minimal adaptive diagnostic baseline, and comparing
against random-policy null processes, we uncover a clear measurement failure:
despite exhibiting deceptively high traditional metrics (e.g., reward fairness of-
ten exceeding 0.9), learned policies perform up to 81% below random baselines
under ALT-variant evaluation—a deficit already present in the two-agent case
and intensifying as n grows.
∗Corresponding author
Email addresses: papadopoulos.nik.al@gmail.com (Nikolaos Al. Papadopoulos),
kpsannis@uom.edu.gr (Konstantinos E. Psannis)
1ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1842-8227
2ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0020-6394
arXiv:2603.05789v1  [cs.MA]  6 Mar 2026


--- Page 2 ---
These results demonstrate, in this setting, that high aggregate payoffs can
coexist with poor temporal coordination, and that conventional metrics may
severely mischaracterize emergent dynamics. Our findings underscore the ne-
cessity of temporally aware observables for analyzing coordination in multi-agent
games and highlight random-policy baselines as essential null processes for in-
terpreting coordination outcomes relative to chance-level behavior.
Keywords:
Fair Division, Multi-Agent Systems, Welfare Measurement,
Turn-Taking, Coordination Dynamics, Markov Games, Cooperative Games
1. Introduction
Coordination among multiple self-interested agents is a fundamental prob-
lem in complex systems, where local decision rules and asymmetric incentives
give rise to emergent collective behavior Rankin et al. (2007); Axelrod (1984);
Perolat et al. (2017). In the absence of centralized control, such systems exhibit
interdependent feedback between individual actions and collective outcomes,
producing diverse long-run coordination regimes Vainer and Kukacka (2021);
Maruta and Okada (2012); Zhu et al. (2020); Shan et al. (2019); He et al.
(2015); Tian et al. (2026); Pikovsky (2009). A central challenge is to character-
ize how efficient or equitable collective patterns emerge—and persist—purely
through repeated interaction, adaptation, and feedback.
Such coordination
problems arise across diverse domains and turn-taking phenomena: decentral-
ized access in wireless networks Shenoy (2007), conversational turn-taking in
linguistics Sacks et al. (1974), animal communication and rhythmic alterna-
tion Takahashi et al. (2013); Greenfield et al. (2021); Ravignani et al. (2019);
Pika et al. (2018), stochastic phenotype switching Rainey et al. (2011), vision-
based robotic coordination Mezey et al. (2025), and emergent conversational
norms in virtual environments Bonfert et al. (2022). Yet existing evaluation
frameworks—including welfare measures from social choice theory and alloca-
tion criteria from fair division—remain largely insensitive to the temporal struc-
ture inherent in these coordination processes.
2


--- Page 3 ---
Repeated interaction among egoistic agents can be naturally viewed as a
discrete-time, stochastic, and history-dependent dynamical system Pikovsky
(2009); Axelrod (1984). Early computational studies, most notably Axelrod’s
investigation of cooperation among egoists without central authority Axelrod
(1984), demonstrated that simple adaptive rules can generate stable behav-
ioral regularities through repetition alone. From a game-theoretic perspective,
learning rules induce adaptive trajectories in a high-dimensional strategy space,
where long-run behavior corresponds to recurrent coordination regimes rather
than one-shot equilibria Pikovsky (2009). Understanding which coordination
patterns emerge, and how to distinguish them quantitatively, remains an open
problem.
The Battle of the Exes (BoE) game provides a minimal yet expressive model
for studying such coordination dynamics Hawkins and Goldstone (2016). Un-
like cooperative dilemmas, BoE is a coordination game with asymmetric payoffs,
where agents must resolve conflict over preferred outcomes. In repeated play, the
socially optimal solution is not static cooperation but temporal alternation, in
which agents take turns accessing the higher-reward outcome Hawkins and Gold-
stone (2016); Papadopoulos and Sanchez-Fibla (2021); Papadopoulos (2020).
This alternation corresponds to a structured temporal pattern—analogous to a
periodic orbit—rather than a simple payoff equilibrium. As such, BoE offers
a natural testbed for examining the emergence of turn-taking conventions as
collective dynamical patterns.
Despite extensive prior work on the repeated BoE, existing studies are al-
most exclusively restricted to the two-agent case Hawkins and Goldstone (2016);
Puig Camps (2018); Gasparrini and Sanchez-Fibla (2018); Freire et al. (2020,
2023); Lopez Hernandez (2022). This limitation obscures coordination phenom-
ena that arise in more realistic multi-agent settings, where competition among
several agents induces richer and higher-dimensional dynamics. Moreover, con-
sidering BoE-derived multi-agent variants exposes a critical methodological gap:
commonly used fairness and efficiency metrics are typically time-averaged and
payoff-based Gini (1912); Theil (1967); Allison (1978); Jiang and Lu (2019); Per-
3


--- Page 4 ---
olat et al. (2017), rendering them insensitive to temporal ordering. As a result,
such metrics cannot distinguish genuine turn-taking from monopolistic, random,
or weakly correlated access patterns, particularly in multi-agent scenarios where
high aggregate efficiency may coexist with poor coordination.
In this study, we address these limitations by formalizing a BoE-derived
multi-agent dynamic Battle of the Exes (MBoE) as a Markov game Littman
(1994). To characterize turn-taking as a temporal phenomenon, we introduce
the concept of a Perfect Alternation (PA) reference coordination regime for
idealized turn-taking. Building on this benchmark, we propose six novel Alter-
nation (ALT) metrics designed as time-series observables that explicitly capture
temporal structure and coordination quality Papadopoulos and Sanchez-Fibla
(2021); Papadopoulos et al. (2025). Through extensive numerical simulations
with Q-learning agents—interpreted here as a discrete stochastic adaptive pro-
cess and used as a minimal learning baseline—we demonstrate that traditional
metrics can mask coordination failure, while ALT metrics reliably distinguish
qualitatively different long-run regimes. Our results highlight the importance of
temporally sensitive observables for analyzing coordination dynamics in strate-
gic multi-agent interaction.
1.1. Main Contributions
The main contributions of this study are as follows:
1. Multi-agent formalization of BoE: We study a BoE-derived multi-
agent variant of the classical two-player Battle of the Exes and formalize
it as a Markov game, enabling the study of high-dimensional coordination
dynamics.
2. Perfect Alternation (PA): We introduce the concept of Perfect Al-
ternation (PA) as a reference coordination regime for turn-taking tasks
in multi-agent systems Papadopoulos (2020); Papadopoulos and Sanchez-
Fibla (2021).
3. Novel Alternation (ALT) metrics: We propose six ALT measures
that overcome the temporal insensitivity and coordination blindness of
4


--- Page 5 ---
traditional fairness and efficiency metrics—their inherent inability to dis-
tinguish structured alternation from monopolistic or random access pat-
terns, even when reporting values approaching 1.0. This dual limitation
becomes severe and misleading as the number of agents grows, with tra-
ditional metrics masking severe coordination failure Papadopoulos and
Sanchez-Fibla (2021); Papadopoulos et al. (2025).
4. ALT ratio benchmarking framework: We develop a regression-based
methodology mapping ALT metric values to Perfect Alternation–equivalent
performance, enabling interpretable quantification of coordination quality.
5. Random baseline as null process: We establish random policy base-
lines as a statistical null hypothesis for coordination, demonstrating that
high fairness or efficiency values can arise purely by chance—a method-
ological practice not consistently reported in prior BoE and closely related
coordination studies Hawkins and Goldstone (2016); Puig Camps (2018);
Gasparrini and Sanchez-Fibla (2018); Freire et al. (2020, 2023).
6. Numerical insights into emergent coordination: Through extensive
Q-learning simulations, we show that efficient and seemingly fair outcomes
may conceal poor coordination, while ALT metrics expose underlying dy-
namical regimes.
1.2. Research Questions
This study focuses on the following research questions:
RQ1: How do conventional fairness and efficiency metrics behave in multi-agent
turn-taking settings, and under what conditions do they fail to distinguish
structured coordination from random or monopolistic dynamics?
RQ2: How effectively do ALT metrics separate coordination regimes in empiri-
cal evaluation (perfect alternation, partial alternation, random access) in
multi-agent BoE?
RQ3: How does coordination quality, as measured by ALT metrics, scale with
the number of agents in the multi-agent Battle of the Exes?
5


--- Page 6 ---
RQ4: How do ALT metrics compare against a random-policy null process, and
what does this comparison reveal about apparent coordination measured
by conventional metrics?
Note: Simple Q-learning is used as a minimal adaptive baseline to generate
coordination dynamics, rather than as a benchmark for optimal learning per-
formance.
1.3. Manuscript Organization
The remainder of the manuscript is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews
the classical Battle of the Exes and introduces the proposed multi-agent vari-
ant. Section 3 discusses traditional fairness and efficiency metrics and motivates
the need for temporally sensitive observables. Section 4 introduces the Perfect
Alternation reference coordination regime and the six ALT metrics. Section 5
presents the numerical experiments and analyzes emergent coordination regimes.
Section 6 discusses the findings and their implications. Section 7 concludes with
key insights, limitations, and directions for future work.
2. Battle of the Exes and Prior Work
2.1. The Battle of the Exes Game
The Battle of the Exes (BoE), introduced by Hawkins and Goldstone (2016),
is a coordination game derived from the classical Battle of Sexes, but with
inverted incentives. Rather than coordinating on the same action, agents must
avoid each other while competing for asymmetric rewards. If both agents select
the same action, no reward is obtained; if they select different actions, each
agent receives a payoff determined by individual preference asymmetry.
Table 1: Two-player Battle of the Exes payoff matrix
Action 1
Action 2
Action 1
(0, 0)
(3, 2)
Action 2
(2, 3)
(0, 0)
6


--- Page 7 ---
BoE exists in two variants: a ballistic and a dynamic formulation. In the bal-
listic version, agents make simultaneous discrete action choices at each round,
analogous to a standard normal-form game. In contrast, the dynamic version
embeds decision-making in continuous space and time: agents move toward pre-
ferred destinations within an environment, and conflicts arise when agents arrive
at the same destination within overlapping time windows. This spatiotemporal
coupling introduces additional feedback channels that facilitate the emergence
of turn-taking behavior in repeated interaction.
BoE admits multiple equilibria in the one-shot game, including pure and
mixed Nash equilibria. However, in repeated interaction the socially optimal
outcome is not static coordination but temporal alternation, where agents take
turns accessing the higher-reward outcome. This alternation corresponds to a
structured temporal pattern, rather than a stationary payoff equilibrium.
Hawkins and Goldstone investigated both ballistic and dynamic versions of
BoE using human participants. Their experiments demonstrated that the dy-
namic formulation substantially increases the emergence of turn-taking behav-
ior, with some pairs converging to near-perfect alternation, highlighting the role
of spatiotemporal interaction in coordination dynamics Hawkins and Goldstone
(2016).
2.2. Learning-Based Studies of BoE
Following these findings, several studies examined whether artificial agents
could reproduce the alternation behavior observed in humans.
Puig Camps
(2018) investigated repeated BoE using independent Q-learning agents and re-
ported convergence to stable deterministic strategies under specific learning-rate
and exploration settings. Gasparrini and Sanchez-Fibla (2018) incorporated loss
aversion into agents’ utility functions, accelerating convergence in the ballistic
formulation and enabling alternation in the dynamic variant. Related modeling
work explored control-based or episodic-memory agents in BoE-like settings,
focusing on matching human efficiency/fairness/stability profiles rather than
multi-agent scaling Freire et al. (2020, 2023); Lopez Hernandez (2022).
7


--- Page 8 ---
Despite these advances, learning-based BoE studies remain almost exclu-
sively restricted to the two-agent setting, where alternation reduces to binary
role switching and random policies can achieve near-perfect Fairness, Reward
Fairness, and TT Fairness, and high efficiency by chance alone.
Evaluation
relies primarily on time-averaged, payoff-based metrics such as efficiency and
fairness, which suffer from temporal insensitivity and pattern ambiguity (ran-
dom access can yield values indistinguishable from coordination) even in dyadic
settings, with additional limitations—coordination blindness and exclusion of
intermediate agents—emerging as n grows. Moreover, random-policy baselines
are not consistently reported as explicit null processes Hawkins and Goldstone
(2016); Puig Camps (2018); Gasparrini and Sanchez-Fibla (2018); Freire et al.
(2020, 2023), making it difficult to assess whether observed coordination reli-
ably exceeds chance-level behavior. An exception is recent MSc thesis work that
includes randomized control comparisons in dyadic settings Lopez Hernandez
(2022); however, this comparison served primarily to validate model perfor-
mance against human data rather than to establish a formal null hypothesis for
multi-agent coordination assessment, leaving open the question of how severely
traditional metrics can obscure coordination failure. Our study addresses these
gaps by (i) formalizing random policies as an explicit statistical null process with
quantitative Coordination Score and Relative Change methodology, (ii) scal-
ing baselines to multi-agent settings (n ∈{2, 3, 5, 8, 10}) across four experi-
mental configurations, four outcome-based metrics, and six ALT variants, and
(iii) demonstrating that traditional metrics can appear deceptively high even
under random play—a finding that motivates the introduction of temporally
sensitive ALT metrics.
Building upon and substantially extending our early
conceptual work on alternation-sensitive metrics and periodicity-based analy-
sis Papadopoulos (2020); Papadopoulos and Sanchez-Fibla (2021); Papadopou-
los et al. (2025), this paper introduces a rigorous theoretical framing, calibrated
random baselines, and scaled multi-agent evaluation with refined PA and metric
definitions.
8


--- Page 9 ---
2.3. Why Multi-Agent BoE Is Fundamentally Different
BoE-derived multi-agent variants introduce qualitatively new coordination
challenges. With n > 2 agents competing simultaneously for access to a sin-
gle high-reward outcome, alternation is no longer binary but requires collective
periodic rotation across all participants.
Partial exclusion, intermittent mo-
nopolization, and irregular access patterns emerge naturally, producing richer
temporal dynamics absent from the two-agent case.
Crucially, this extension exposes a methodological gap: conventional fairness
and efficiency metrics scale poorly with agent count and remain insensitive to
temporal ordering. High efficiency may result from persistent monopolization
or random access, while fairness ratios often collapse to extreme values that
obscure intermediate agents. Consequently, even severe coordination failures
can appear successful under traditional evaluation.
2.4. Battle of the Exes as a Minimal Multi-Agent MARL Testbed
To address these limitations, we formalize a Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes
(MBoE) as a BoE-derived multi-agent variant in the form of an episodic Markov
game involving n ≥2 self-interested agents. In each episode, agents act simul-
taneously and compete to reach a terminal state associated with a shared high
reward. If exactly one agent succeeds, it receives the maximum payoff; if mul-
tiple but not all agents succeed, they receive reduced rewards; if all agents tie,
no reward is assigned. We use a minimal learning setting (tabular Q-learning),
a minimal dynamic formulation (single pre-terminal position), and a minimal
environment design to maximize interpretability.
This BoE-derived formulation preserves the core incentive structure of BoE—
mutual interference with asymmetric rewards—while providing a plausible multi-
agent variant. The optimal collective behavior is no longer simple avoidance but
periodic turn-taking across all agents. Whether and how such behavior emerges
under decentralized learning—and how it should be evaluated—are the central
questions addressed in the remainder of this manuscript.
9


--- Page 10 ---
In the following sections, we demonstrate that existing fairness and effi-
ciency metrics are fundamentally inadequate for characterizing multi-agent turn-
taking. We therefore introduce Perfect Alternation as a reference coordination
regime and propose novel, temporally sensitive Alternation (ALT) metrics ca-
pable of distinguishing genuine coordination from spurious access patterns.
2.5. Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes as a Markov Game
We now formally define the Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes (MBoE) as an
episodic Markov game Littman (1994). This BoE-derived formulation consti-
tutes a core contribution of this work, providing a plausible multi-agent variant
of the classical two-agent coordination tension—mutual interference with asym-
metric payoffs.
2.5.1. Game Definition
The MBoE is defined as a Markov game
G = (N, S, A, T, R),
(1)
where N denotes the set of agents, S the state space, A the joint action space,
T the transition function, and R the reward function.
The game is episodic: each episode begins from a fixed initial configuration
and terminates when at least one agent reaches a terminal state.
2.5.2. Agents
Let
N = {1, 2, . . . , n},
n ≥2,
(2)
denote the set of self-interested agents. All agents act simultaneously and inde-
pendently at each decision step and aim to maximize their individual cumulative
reward.
10


--- Page 11 ---
2.5.3. Action Space
At each time step t, each agent i ∈N selects an action
ai
t ∈A = {move, stay}.
(3)
The joint action is denoted by at = (a1
t, . . . , an
t ).
2.5.4. Transition Dynamics
Agents progress along individual paths of finite length toward terminal out-
comes. The transition function
T : S × A →S
(4)
is deterministic and updates each agent’s position according to
pi
t+1 =





pi
t + 1,
if ai
t = move,
pi
t,
if ai
t = stay.
(5)
Positions are non-decreasing, and agents may advance by at most one posi-
tion per time step. An episode terminates as soon as at least one agent reaches
the terminal position.
2.5.5. Reward Function
Rewards are assigned only at terminal states. Let
k =
n
X
i=1
I(pi
T = terminal)
(6)
denote the number of agents reaching the terminal position at the end of an
episode. The reward for agent i is given by
Ri(sT , aT ) =











rhigh,
if k = 1 and agent i succeeds alone,
rlow,
if 1 < k < n and agent i succeeds,
0,
otherwise.
(7)
If all agents reach the terminal position simultaneously (k = n), no reward
is assigned. This reward structure is inspired by the canonical two-agent Bat-
tle of the Exes, preserving its core coordination tension: agents benefit from
11


--- Page 12 ---
exclusive access but suffer when competing for the same outcome. While the
canonical two-agent BoE is defined over asymmetric destination preferences,
the present formulation abstracts this structure into a single contested terminal
with exclusivity-based rewards. This abstraction preserves the essential coordi-
nation tension—mutual interference under asymmetric benefit—while enabling
systematic scaling to the multi-agent regime.
For instance, in a 3-agent system with rhigh = 100: if agent 1 wins alone,
R1 = 100 and R2 = R3 = 0; if agents 1 and 2 both reach the terminal (partial
tie), R1 = R2 = rlow and R3 = 0; if all three agents tie, R1 = R2 = R3 = 0.
2.5.6. Coordination Structure
In the multi-agent setting, optimal collective behavior corresponds to pe-
riodic turn-taking, where agents alternate access to the high-reward outcome
across episodes. This structured temporal pattern forms the basis for the Per-
fect Alternation reference coordination regime introduced in Section 4.
3. Traditional Multi-Agent Fairness Measures
Multi-agent fairness research distinguishes between outcome fairness (cu-
mulative payoff distribution) and procedural fairness (temporal distribution of
rewards). Common outcome measures include the Gini coefficient Gini (1912),
Theil index Theil (1967), and Coefficient of Variation Allison (1978), while re-
cent multi-agent work often uses payoff-based fairness or equality variants Jiang
and Lu (2019); Perolat et al. (2017). In the fair division literature, allocation
mechanisms are evaluated by properties such as envy-freeness, proportionality,
and strategyproofness Suksompong and Teh (2023); Camacho et al. (2023), yet
these criteria remain distributional and do not account for the temporal sequence
of allocations. Similarly, axiomatic approaches to cooperative surplus sharing
emphasize distributional fairness and consistency Calleja and Llerena (2020),
but do not address the temporal ordering of payoffs. Consequently, these met-
rics fail to capture when agents receive rewards—critical for turn-taking tasks
12


--- Page 13 ---
where an agent monopolizing early episodes violates fairness even if cumula-
tive payoffs equalize. These shortcomings already appear in the two-agent BoE
setting; see Supplementary Section S3 for extended discussion. This limitation
motivates the alternation-sensitive measures developed in Section 4.
3.1. Limitations of Traditional Metrics in BoE and MBoE
We examine four traditional metrics and their shortcomings for the Battle
of the Exes (BoE) and its multi-agent variant (MBoE).
Fairness.. Defined as F ν = mini wi/ maxi wi.
Key limitations: (1) ignores
intermediate agents (only min/max), (2) undefined when all episodes are ties
(0/0), and (3) identical values arise from fair rotation, subgroup monopoly, or
random access.
Efficiency.. Defined as Eν = P
i rtotal
i
/(ν × rhigh), measuring total reward cap-
ture. Key limitations: (1) high efficiency can result from monopoly or alterna-
tion, and (2) provides no information about coordination patterns.
Turn-Taking Fairness.. Defined as F T T
ν
= mini ti/ maxi ti Hawkins and Gold-
stone (2016). Key limitations: (1) still ignores intermediate agents, and (2) high
ti can reflect frequent collisions rather than genuine alternation.
Reward Fairness.. Defined as F R
ν
= mini pi/ maxi pi Hawkins and Goldstone
(2016); Puig Camps (2018); Gasparrini and Sanchez-Fibla (2018), comparing
cumulative payoffs. Key limitations: (1) high values can result from tie rewards
without turn-taking, and (2) a dominant subgroup can still yield high Reward
Fairness.
Here, wi counts exclusive wins, ti counts terminal arrivals (including ties), and
pi is cumulative payoff for agent i over ν episodes.
Combining Efficiency with Fairness or TT Fairness does not even guarantee
a fair outcome interpretation: Fairness ignores intermediate agents, while TT
Fairness conflates exclusive wins with collision-induced arrivals. Only Reward
Fairness, when combined with high Efficiency, can indicate equitable payoff
13


--- Page 14 ---
Table 2: Diagnostic limitations of traditional metric combinations. Each pairing was system-
atically evaluated across all nine value combinations arising from Efficiency ∈{0, (0, 1), 1}
and the respective Fairness variant ∈{0, (0, 1), 1}. Temporal coordination patterns remain
invisible in all cases; fair outcome interpretation is possible only for Reward Fairness at the
optimal corner (Efficiency = 1, Reward Fairness = 1). Complete cell-by-cell analysis appears
in Supplementary Material §3.2.5.
Metric Pair
Temporal
Fair Outcome
(with Efficiency)
Structure
Confirmed?
Fairness
None
No
(ignores
intermediate
agents)
Turn-Taking Fairness
None
No (conflates collisions with
turns)
Reward Fairness
None
Only at (Eff=1, RF=1)
distribution—but even then, temporal structure remains invisible. This double
inadequacy—failing both as fairness indicators and as coordination measures—
motivates the development of ALT metrics.
Table 2 illustrates that inter-
pretable patterns emerge only at extremes; at intermediate values—common
in practice—these metrics fail to distinguish coordination quality.
Random-
policy baselines are not consistently reported as explicit null references in prior
BoE-related studies Hawkins and Goldstone (2016); Puig Camps (2018); Gas-
parrini and Sanchez-Fibla (2018); Freire et al. (2020, 2023); we therefore include
them as an explicit null reference for coordination assessment.
4. Proposed Assessment Framework
Traditional metrics fail to capture temporal structure—how access to high-
payoff states is distributed across agents over time. We introduce Alternation
Measures (ALT) that explicitly quantify who succeeds, when, and in what se-
quence.
4.1. Perfect Alternation as Reference
Perfect Alternation (PA) is a coordination regime where each of n agents
reaches the highest-payoff state exactly once within every block of n episodes,
14


--- Page 15 ---
in any order.
Stricter order-sensitive variants (e.g., requiring each agent to
occupy each rank equally often across batches) are possible but not imposed
here. PA can be used descriptively, normatively, or prescriptively depending
on the application; here it serves only as a reference regime against which ob-
served coordination can be compared Papadopoulos (2020); Papadopoulos and
Sanchez-Fibla (2021). Human participants in BoE experiments sometimes con-
verge to PA-like patterns Hawkins and Goldstone (2016), motivating its use as a
meaningful boundary condition. PA is Pareto-optimal under strict rotation, and
in the two-agent ballistic case it constitutes a Nash equilibrium; detailed equilib-
rium links and the three motivating reasons (empirical observability, temporal
sensitivity, metric calibration) are provided in Supplementary Section S4.
4.2. ALT Framework
Given ν episodes and n agents, ALT metrics evaluate all b = ν −(n −1)
overlapping batches of n consecutive episodes. Each batch j receives a weight
βj ∈[0, 1], where βj = 1 indicates perfect alternation. The overall score averages
batch weights:
ALT = 1
b
b
X
j=1
βj
(8)
ALT measures satisfy: (i) boundedness in [0, 1]; (ii) ALT = 1 under PA;
(iii) monotonic degradation under monopolization or ties; (iv) sensitivity to
exclusivity; (v) permutation invariance Papadopoulos and Sanchez-Fibla (2021);
Papadopoulos et al. (2025). For this study, order within a batch is not penalized;
any permutation is treated as ideal. Full symbol definitions and computation
details are provided in Supplementary Section S4.
4.3. Six ALT Variants
We define six variants spanning tolerant to strict formulations. Let fj =
unique winners in batch j, τj = total terminal occurrences, wj = episodes with
exclusive winners, gj = agents with exactly one exclusive win, and Yℓ= agents
reaching terminal in episode ℓ.
15


--- Page 16 ---
FALT (Fractional).. Most tolerant; measures unique winners relative to termi-
nal occurrences:
βFALT
j
= fj
τj
(9)
qFALT (Quadratic Fractional).. More sensitive (quadratic penalty):3
βqFALT
j
=
␀βFALT
j
2
(10)
EALT (Exclusive).. Emphasizes exclusivity—episodes with single winners:
βEALT
j
= wj · fj
n2
(11)
qEALT (Quadratic Exclusive).. More sensitive exclusive measure (quadratic
penalty):4
βqEALT
j
=
␀βEALT
j
2
(12)
CALT (Complete).. Primary metric; penalizes ties explicitly:
βCALT
j
=
Pn
ℓ=1(n −Yℓ) · βqFALT
j
n(n −1)
(13)
AALT (Absolute).. One of the strictest; rewards only agents with exactly one
exclusive win per batch:
βAALT
j
= gj
τj
(14)
CALT serves as the primary metric due to its balanced treatment of exclu-
sivity and ties. FALT provides a tolerant baseline, EALT isolates exclusivity,
and AALT sets a strict diagnostic bound. ALT measures complement—rather
than replace—traditional metrics by capturing temporal coordination invisible
to outcome-based evaluation.
3Previously called EFALT; renamed since the operation is quadratic, not exponential.
4Previously called EEALT.
16


--- Page 17 ---
5. Experimental Setup and Results
5.1. Environment and Experimental Design
We study MBoE coordination using two state representations: Type-A
(position-only: sA
t = (p1
t, . . . , pn
t )) and Type-B (with episodic memory: sB
t =
((p1
t, . . . , pn
t ), (z1
t−1, . . . , zn
t−1)), where zi
t−1 = 1 if agent i won the previous
episode). Two reward schemes scale tie penalties: ILF (rlow = rhigh/n) and
IQF (rlow = rhigh/n2), with rhigh = 100.5
Experiments span all combinations of state types (Type-A/B), reward schemes
(ILF/IQF), and agent counts (n ∈{2, 3, 5, 8, 10}), yielding 20 configurations.
Code is available at https://github.com/dentros/Alternation (GNU GPL).
5.2. Q-Learning and Training
Each agent maintains an independent tabular Q-table with no inter-agent
communication. Hyperparameters: γ = 0.999, α = 0.3, ϵ-greedy with linear
decay from ϵinitial = 0.9 to ϵmin = 0.004. Q-learning was selected over deep RL
for complete interpretability of coordination dynamics.
Episode counts scale with agent complexity:
Episodes(n) = BASE ×
n
2
2
×

1 + ln
n!
2!

(15)
with BASE=1000, yielding: 1,000 (2 agents), 4,721 (3), 31,839 (5), 174,583 (8),
and 385,281 (10 agents) episodes per configuration. This schedule is heuris-
tic, chosen to balance state-space growth against computational and energy
constraints; pilot runs with alternative scalings produced qualitatively similar
trends.
5.3. AltRatio and PA-Equivalent Interpretation
To interpret ALT values, we define AltRatio = x/n ∈[0, 1], where x repre-
sents the equivalent number of perfectly alternating agents, while the remaining
n −x agents stay stationary.
Multiplying by n yields the PA-equivalent:
5Previously called EFR/EAFR; renamed to reflect inverse polynomial operations.
17


--- Page 18 ---
“This system coordinates as well as x out of n perfectly alternating agents.”
Benchmarking details and regression equations appear in Supplementary Sec-
tion S1. PA-equivalent is an interpretive mapping distinct from Relative Change
and Coordination Score, which compare observed ALT values to random-policy
baselines.
5.4. Random Policy Baselines
For each configuration, we ran 10,000 episodes with uniformly random ac-
tions to establish chance-level performance. Coordination is quantified via:
Relative Change = ALTobserved −ALTrandom
ALTrandom
× 100,
Coordination Score = ALTobserved −ALTrandom
ALTperfect −ALTrandom
× 100
(16)
where ALTperfect = 1.0. Negative scores indicate performance worse than ran-
dom. Random policies achieve deceptively high traditional metrics (Efficiency
≈0.82, Fairness ≈0.97 for 2 agents) but low ALT values (CALT: 0.486 to 0.111
across agent counts).
5.5. Results
5.5.1. Random Baseline Performance
Table 3 presents comprehensive random baseline results across all agent con-
figurations. The key finding is that random policies achieve deceptively
high traditional metrics, creating an illusion of coordination:
For 2-agent systems, random policies achieve Efficiency = 0.818 and Fairness
= 0.972—values that would typically be interpreted as indicating successful
coordination. Even with 5 agents, random policies maintain Efficiency ≥0.532
and Fairness ≥0.927. These high traditional metric values persist despite the
complete absence of any learned coordination strategy.
In stark contrast, ALT metrics properly expose the lack of coordination in
random policies. CALT values decrease systematically as agent count increases:
from 0.486 (2 agents) to 0.359 (3 agents), 0.243 (5 agents), 0.147 (8 agents),
and finally 0.111 (10 agents). This monotonic decrease reflects the increasing
18


--- Page 19 ---
Figure 1: Q-Learning vs Random Baseline CALT Performance. Blue bars show Q-learning
agents consistently achieving lower CALT values than random baselines (orange bars) across
all agent configurations (2, 3, 5, 8, 10 agents). All coordination scores are negative, indicating
Q-learning performs worse than chance. Error bars represent standard deviation across Type-
A/Type-B and ILF/IQF configurations.
difficulty of achieving coordination by chance alone as the number of competing
agents grows.
Figure 1 visualizes this critical comparison, showing that Q-learning agents
(blue bars) consistently achieve lower CALT values than random baselines (or-
ange bars) across all agent configurations—a finding that challenges assumptions
about learned coordination in this class of multi-agent systems. We emphasize
that this result does not generalize to all MARL algorithms, but highlights a
failure mode of independent tabular learners under temporal coordination de-
mands. We report CALT explicitly as the primary alternation metric; other
ALT variants are summarized in Table 4.
5.5.2. Q-Learning Coordination Failure
Our Q-learning experiments reveal that, despite achieving high traditional
fairness and efficiency metrics, Q-learning agents demonstrate systematically
worse coordination than random policies in this setting. Table 4 reports
both Relative Change and Coordination Score for FALT, EALT, CALT,
19


--- Page 20 ---
Figure 2: Perfect Alternation Equivalent Performance Degradation. Q-learning agents (blue
line; mean across 4 modes) decline from ∼56.4% (2 agents) to ∼17.9% (10 agents) of perfect
coordination, while random baselines (orange line) decline from 69.7% to 33.3%.
Shaded
regions indicate 95% confidence intervals.
Q-learning remains below random across agent
counts, indicating persistent coordination deficits.
and AALT:
Key Finding: For FALT/EALT/CALT/AALT, all Coordination Scores
are negative, indicating performance below random baselines after normalization
to perfect alternation. Relative Change is negative for all ALT variants except
10-agent qEALT (+5.4%), where more frequent multi-agent ties yield non-zero
rewards and behavior drifts toward the random baseline; the quadratic form
accentuates small gains and tracks the slightly higher efficiency in that regime
(see Supplementary Section S6).
The coordination failure is most severe for specific configurations:
• 5 agents, qEALT: -81.2% (worst performance)
• 5 agents, AALT: -79.6% (near-worst)
• 10 agents, CALT: -56.6% (severe degradation)
Meanwhile, traditional metrics mask this coordination failure completely.
For the same experiments, Q-learning agents achieve:
20

0.111
0.363

0.220
0.846
Note: Random policies achieve deceptively high traditional metrics (Efficiency
≥0.443 for n ≤10, Fairness ≥0.816), creating an illusion of coordination. In
contrast, ALT metrics properly expose lack of coordination, with CALT
dropping from 0.486 (2 agents) to 0.111 (10 agents).
• Reward Fairness: 0.490–0.993 (moderate to near-perfect)
• Efficiency: 0.054–0.677 (low to moderate)
• TT Fairness: 0.699–0.998 (moderate to near-perfect)
These traditional metric values would conventionally be interpreted as suc-
cessful multi-agent coordination.
Our ALT metrics reveal the opposite: Q-
learning agents have learned policies that are actively worse than random action
selection for achieving turn-taking coordination in this setting.
Figure 3 illustrates this dichotomy, showing high traditional metrics along-
side low ALT metrics, demonstrating how conventional measures fail to detect
coordination failure in MBoE scenarios.
21


--- Page 22 ---
5.5.3. Perfect Alternation Equivalent Analysis
To provide interpretable quantification of coordination quality, we compute
PA-equivalent performance using the AltRatio framework. Table 5 presents this
analysis for CALT:
Table 5 reports reward-type averages, whereas Figure 2 visualizes the mean
± range across the four modes (Type-A/B × ILF/IQF).
The PA-equivalent analysis reveals sharp degradation from small to mid
agent counts and a low plateau thereafter:
• 2 agents: 56.8% of perfect coordination (1.14 equiv. agents)
• 3 agents: 37.7% of perfect coordination (1.13 equiv. agents)
• 5 agents: 25.0% of perfect coordination (1.25 equiv. agents)
• 8 agents: 21.6% of perfect coordination (1.73 equiv. agents)
• 10 agents: 21.9% of perfect coordination (2.19 equiv. agents)
Performance drops sharply from 56.8% (2 agents) to 25.0% (5 agents) and
remains near ∼22% for 8–10 agents, demonstrating that coordination difficulty
scales sharply with system size. Notably, 10 Q-learning agents achieve coordi-
nation equivalent to only 2.19 perfectly alternating agents—barely one-fifth of
the agents in the system exhibiting coordinated behavior.
Figure 2 visualizes this degradation trend, showing how PA-equivalent per-
formance decreases as agent count increases.
5.5.4. Symmetric Coordination Failure
A particularly illuminating case study emerges from comparing 3-agent con-
figurations under different state representations (Figure 4). Despite nearly iden-
tical high traditional metrics (Reward Fairness > 0.90), the systems exhibit sim-
ilar low ALT performance (CALT ≈0.14). This symmetric failure across state
representations and reward structures suggests that the coordination deficit is
fundamental to the Q-learning approach in MBoE, rather than an artifact of
specific experimental design choices.
22


--- Page 23 ---
Figure 3: Traditional Metrics Fail to Detect Coordination Failure. Left panels show tradi-
tional outcome metrics achieving moderate-to-high values across configurations, suggesting
successful coordination. Right panels show alternation-sensitive metrics exposing poor coor-
dination, with all values below random baselines (dashed lines). This dichotomy demonstrates
that traditional outcome-based fairness measures cannot distinguish coordinated alternation
from monopolistic or random resource access.
23


--- Page 24 ---
Figure 4: Symmetric Coordination Failure in 3-Agent Systems. Despite nearly identical high
traditional metrics (Reward Fairness > 0.90, Efficiency > 0.34), both Type-A ILF and Type-B
IQF configurations exhibit similarly low ALT performance (CALT ≈0.14). This symmetric
failure across state representations and reward structures demonstrates that coordination
deficit is fundamental to independent Q-learning, not an artifact of specific design choices.
Figure 5: CALT Progression During Q-Learning Training. CALT values (solid lines) decrease
during learning as epsilon decays (dashed line, right axis), indicating that learned policies
achieve worse coordination than early exploration phases. Traditional efficiency (dotted lines)
follows a different trend over training and does not track CALT. This finding suggests that
convergence to deterministic policies can interfere with alternation while traditional metrics
may suggest improvement.
24


--- Page 25 ---

Interpretation: The ALT Ratio maps observed CALT values to equivalent perfectly
alternating agents. For example, 10 Q-learning agents achieve CALT = 0.048,
equivalent to only 2.19 perfectly alternating agents (21.9% of perfect coordination).
PA-Equivalent Formula (CALT): ALT Ratio =
√
CALT −1.879 × 10−10
PA Equivalent Agents = n × ALT Ratio
Key Trend: Performance drops sharply from 2 to 5 agents and remains low for 8–10
agents, demonstrating coordination difficulty scales dramatically with system size.
26


--- Page 27 ---
6. Discussion
6.1. Why Does Q-Learning Fail at Coordination?
Our finding—that independent Q-learning agents perform below random
baselines—demands explanation. We identify four interrelated factors:
1. The Credit Assignment Problem: Tabular Q-learning cannot rec-
ognize that “losing now” (allowing another agent to win) yields “winning later”
(reciprocal cooperation). Without function approximation or opponent mod-
eling, agents treat each episode independently, missing the temporal structure
of alternation equilibria. The reward for cooperation materializes n episodes in
the future, longer than the typical effective temporal credit assignment horizon
in tabular Q-learning with γ = 0.999.
2. Non-Stationary Opponent Dynamics: From each agent’s perspec-
tive, other agents’ evolving policies violate assumptions underlying standard
convergence results for Q-learning. As opponents explore different strategies
during early training, the environment appears stochastic and non-stationary.
Once all agents simultaneously cease exploration (synchronized epsilon decay
at 75% of episodes), they may converge to a suboptimal coordination regime
(Nash-like, locally stable) where no single agent can unilaterally improve by
alternating—a classic coordination failure Masili¯unas (2019).
This synchro-
nized decay may also contribute to mid-range bumps in EALT/qEALT: partial
exploration increases exclusive wins before late-stage exploitation drives more
simultaneous arrivals and ties.
3. Lack of Explicit Coordination Signals: Unlike humans who achieve
PA through implicit signaling (e.g., “I won last time, your turn now”), inde-
pendent learners have no communication channel. State representations lack
sufficient context to infer “whose turn it is,” particularly for n > 3 agents where
alternation cycles exceed the single-episode memory of Type-B states.
4. Tragedy of the Learning Commons: Each agent learns to exploit op-
portunities for rhigh greedily, maximizing individual expected return. Without
global coordination mechanisms, this individual rationality leads to collective
27


--- Page 28 ---
irrationality—agents interfere with each other’s learning trajectories, producing
worse outcomes than random policies that at least distribute wins stochastically.
These factors compound as agent count increases, explaining the sharp drop
in PA-equivalent performance from 56.8% (2 agents) to 25.0% (5 agents) and
the low plateau around ∼22% for 8–10 agents. The coordination problem is
not merely difficult—it is poorly aligned with the assumptions of independent
tabular reinforcement learning.
6.2. Coordination Breakdown as Strategic Regime Shift
The performance drop from 56.8% (2 agents) to ∼22% (8–10 agents) reflects
a fundamental shift in the equilibrium structure of multi-agent games: as agent
count increases, the strategic complexity of achieving coordinated alternation
grows sharply, and independent learners fail to discover equilibria supporting
fair turn-taking Pikovsky (2009); Maruta and Okada (2012); Masili¯unas (2019);
Zhu et al. (2020); Shan et al. (2019); He et al. (2015); Tian et al. (2026). Below
a certain agent threshold, residual coordination persists through partial turn-
taking patterns; beyond this threshold, individual rationality leads to collective
irrationality—agents interfere with each other’s strategies, producing coordina-
tion outcomes qualitatively close to random dynamics.
The finding that 10 Q-learning agents coordinate as effectively as only 2.19
perfectly alternating agents constitutes a notable finding, not merely a negative
result. It identifies a regime in which coordination breaks down in decentral-
ized multi-agent systems—information that remains entirely hidden when using
traditional metrics whose ranges can appear deceptively high (e.g., Reward Fair-
ness up to 0.993 and Efficiency up to 0.677). Such breakdowns, where individual
optimization undermines collective welfare, are central concerns in game theory,
mechanism design, and the study of social dilemmas.
This perspective reframes our experimental results: rather than demon-
strating algorithmic failure, they reveal constraints on emergent coordination
in systems where individual optimization conflicts with collective welfare. Per-
formance remains low and close to random between 8 and 10 agents, with only a
28


--- Page 29 ---
slight uptick at 10 agents consistent with higher tie frequency and quadratic am-
plification in qEALT, suggesting a near-random regime that merits finer-grained
agent-count sweeps.
6.3. Implications for Random Baseline Methodology
A practical implication of our results is that coordination metrics should be
interpreted relative to an explicit null process. Random-policy baselines provide
a simple and transparent null hypothesis for “no temporal coordination”: they
quantify the level of efficiency, fairness, and alternation that can arise purely
from chance under the same environment and episode budget.
In prior work on the Battle of the Exes and related repeated coordination
games, random-policy baselines are not consistently reported as a standard ref-
erence, and in several cases the presence or absence of such baselines is not
discussed explicitly Hawkins and Goldstone (2016); Puig Camps (2018); Gas-
parrini and Sanchez-Fibla (2018); Freire et al. (2020, 2023). This can make it
difficult to assess whether observed coordination reliably exceeds chance-level
behavior, particularly when outcome-based metrics take deceptively high values
even under random play.
Additional discussion on interpretability and reconciliation with prior work
is provided in Supplementary Section S6.
7. Conclusions
This study introduces Perfect Alternation (PA) as a reference coordina-
tion regime and limiting benchmark for temporal turn-taking, alongside six
novel ALT metrics and a regression-based benchmarking framework enabling
interpretable quantification of coordination quality. Through comprehensive Q-
learning experiments with rigorous random policy baselines—a methodological
practice not consistently reported in prior BoE-related studies—we reveal fun-
damental limitations in both traditional evaluation metrics and learned coordi-
nation strategies Hawkins and Goldstone (2016); Puig Camps (2018); Gasparrini
and Sanchez-Fibla (2018); Freire et al. (2020, 2023).
29


--- Page 30 ---
7.1. Key Findings
Our experimental results challenge conventional assumptions about multi-
agent coordination in three critical ways:
1. Traditional Metrics Severely Fail to Detect Coordination Fail-
ure. Across all configurations, Q-learning agents exhibit Reward Fairness in
the range 0.49–0.993, Efficiency 0.054–0.677, and TT Fairness 0.699–0.998—
values that can appear deceptively high in aggregate. However, ALT metrics
reveal the opposite: Relative Change reaches as low as -81% (qEALT), with only
one small positive at +5.4% for 10-agent qEALT, demonstrating that learned
policies perform worse than random baselines under ALT evaluation. This di-
chotomy exposes a fundamental inadequacy in outcome-based fairness measures,
which cannot distinguish monopolistic, random, or alternating resource access
patterns in temporal multi-agent scenarios.
2. Q-Learning Produces Systematic Under-Coordination in Multi-
Agent BoE. With only one negligible exception (10-agent qEALT at +5.4%),
all coordination scores are negative. This systematic underperformance relative
to random baselines (to our knowledge, not previously established in BoE litera-
ture) indicates that independent Q-learning agents do not merely fail to achieve
coordination—they learn policies that do not promote alternation. The coordi-
nation deficit is substantial across sizes (CALT coordination scores: -33.7% for
2 agents, -74.3% for 5 agents, -56.6% for 10 agents), with AALT and qEALT
confirming this pattern across the full ALT strictness spectrum, suggesting that
tabular Q-learning’s inability to model joint action spaces becomes increasingly
problematic as system complexity grows.
3. Coordination Difficulty scales sharply with Agent Count. PA-
equivalent analysis shows a steep drop from 56.8% of perfect coordination (2
agents) to 25.0% (5 agents) and a low plateau around ∼22% for 8–10 agents.
This indicates that coordination challenges compound non-linearly as competi-
tive pressure increases. The finding that 10 agents achieve coordination equiv-
alent to only 2.19 perfectly alternating agents—representing about one-fifth of
the system—quantifies the severity of the multi-agent coordination problem in
30


--- Page 31 ---
decentralized learning scenarios.
7.2. Theoretical and Methodological Contributions
Perfect Alternation (PA) provides a Pareto-optimal reference point for
evaluating turn-taking coordination, analogous to how optimal solutions serve
as benchmarks in optimization literature. Unlike irregular or group-based coor-
dination patterns, PA ensures fixed periodicity and temporal fairness regardless
of episode count or agent population, making it applicable to indefinite-horizon
scenarios common in real-world resource allocation.
ALT Metrics address critical gaps in existing fairness literature by inte-
grating distributional fairness (min/max ratios) with temporal fairness (rotation
periodicity). The six metrics offer tunable strictness—from FALT’s lenient frac-
tional evaluation to AALT’s exclusive-win requirement—enabling researchers to
select appropriate sensitivity levels for different experimental phases or appli-
cation domains. Critically, these metrics properly expose coordination failure
that traditional measures mask, as demonstrated by our random baseline com-
parisons.
AltRatio Benchmarking Framework enables interpretable quantifica-
tion by mapping ALT values to "PA-equivalent agents" through regression equa-
tions derived from extreme-case simulations (n ∈[2, 40]). This methodology
transforms abstract metric values into actionable statements: "System X coor-
dinates as well as σ perfectly alternating agents." The framework generalizes
beyond current metrics, providing a foundation for developing measures for hi-
erarchical alternation, weighted distribution, or other coordination regimes.
Random Policy Baseline Methodology provides an explicit statistical
null hypothesis for temporal coordination: chance-level behavior under the same
environment and evaluation protocol. Because outcome-based metrics can take
deceptively high values even under random play, reporting random-policy base-
lines offers a minimal but crucial reference for interpreting whether coordination
genuinely exceeds chance-level performance. In our setting, this reference is de-
cisive: for 10 agents, random policies attain Efficiency in the range 0.22–0.44 and
31


--- Page 32 ---
Fairness ≈0.846, while ALT metrics correctly reflect the absence of structured
turn-taking.
While random baselines are widely used in other areas of empirical evalua-
tion, they have not been consistently adopted as standard practice in studies of
the Battle of the Exes and related coordination games, limiting comparability
across works and complicating claims of emergent coordination Hawkins and
Goldstone (2016); Puig Camps (2018); Gasparrini and Sanchez-Fibla (2018);
Freire et al. (2020, 2023).
7.3. Implications, Limitations, and Future Directions
Our results highlight constraints of independent tabular learning for tempo-
ral coordination and point to promising directions, including richer state repre-
sentations, explicit communication mechanisms, and centralized training with
decentralized execution. Extended implications, limitations, future directions,
and application contexts are provided in Supplementary Section S6.
We re-
peated runs with different random seeds and observed qualitatively consistent
patterns; for brevity, we report representative results.
By establishing rigorous baselines, developing interpretable benchmarking
methodologies, and exposing limitations in conventional evaluation practices,
this work advances both theoretical understanding and practical assessment of
multi-agent coordination. More broadly, our findings contribute to the social
choice and welfare measurement literature by demonstrating that distributional
fairness criteria—widely used in fair division and cooperative game theory—can
be fundamentally misleading in repeated interaction settings where the tempo-
ral sequence of allocations matters as much as their aggregate distribution. The
result that Q-learning agents perform below random baselines—despite appear-
ing successful by traditional measures—underscores the necessity of domain-
appropriate metrics and the dangers of relying on outcome fairness alone when
procedural fairness matters.
32


--- Page 33 ---
Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted Technologies in the
Writing Process
During preparation of this manuscript, the authors used AI tools (Claude
3.7/4.5; ChatGPT 5.2) for language editing, formatting, and code organization.
The authors reviewed and edited all outputs and take full responsibility for
the content. The use of these tools did not influence the scientific findings or
conclusions.
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Nikolaos Al. Papadopoulos: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software,
Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visual-
ization. Konstantinos E. Psannis: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
Declaration of competing interest
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or
personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported
in this paper.
Data availability
The code and data supporting the findings of this study are available at
https://github.com/dentros/Alternation (GNU GPL). Archived version:
https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18528890.
Funding
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the
public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
